title: Preop Clearance Dependency Scheduling
description: A scheduled-care coordination workflow that aligns appointments, dependencies, and staff/resource availability across clinical units. Focus case: preop clearance dependency scheduling.
assumptions: Each reservation predicate models an exclusive hold on a resource or slot for the duration required by the workflow step; toggles correctly represent release/restore semantics.
Clinical teams (T15/T16) are distinct and non-overlapping for responsibilities; a case may be linked to multiple teams but team subtypes have separate roles.
Temporal constraints (durations, overlapping windows) are abstracted: slots and availability predicates encode availability but not continuous time semantics.
Human confirmation steps (clinician signoff, nurse checks) are modeled as discrete boolean flags; downstream automation assumes those flags reflect completed real-world tasks.
risks: Predicate naming heterogeneity (resource-first '_assigned' vs case-first '_linked' vs noun 'reservation') creates risk of conceptual conflation when implementing mappings; maintain strict separation of assignment vs association semantics.

Ambiguity between technician-like roles: diagnostic_technician vs generic technician/nurse may be misinterpreted; mapping must preserve operational distinctions (diagnostic procedure performer vs checklist executor).
Resource contention and race conditions are represented only via simple reserve/consume predicates; real-world concurrency and transient availability may not be fully captured.

Subtype distinctions (case_type_elective vs case_type_urgent) are present but timeline/priority behaviours are not modeled; operational policies for priority handling are therefore external to the template.
Assuming exclusivity of slot/resource predicates may fail for shared-resource scenarios (e.g., overlapping non-exclusive equipment use) unless additional modeling is introduced.
